title: Validated Learning

content.statement: Progress is measured in validated learning, not in features shipped.
content.question: What hypothesis did I validate or invalidate this week?
content.equation: Validated Learning = Hypotheses Tested × Real Behavior Observed × Significance
content.mental_model: Lab notebook, what did you prove or disprove this week?
content.business_lens: Track learning per week as a KPI; reward teams that disprove assumptions cheaply.
content.life_lens: Ask weekly: what did you actually learn that changes your plan?
content.explainer: Many startups confuse vanity metrics (page views, downloads) with progress. Validated learning forces specificity: did the customer behave the way the hypothesis predicted? If not, the plan needs to change.
questions: What hypothesis did I validate or invalidate this week?
Look at your last three claimed 'learnings.' Were they real disproving of a stated hypothesis, or just things that confirmed what you already believed?
If you had to bet money on your top assumption being wrong, would you take that bet? What does your answer say about how validated it really is?

definitions.def: A number that goes up and feels good but doesn't predict whether the business model actually works, like downloads or page views.

content.equation_example: 3 hypotheses tested (count) times 40 real customer behaviors observed (count) times significance rated 7 out of 10. Score = 3 times 40 times 7 = 840, scaled to a Validated Learning score of 84. Higher/rising means your conclusions rest on real customer action, not opinions; lower/falling means you're calling a small or biased sample 'learning.'
content.closing_statement: A confirmed belief isn't the same as a validated one.

caption:
Principle Nº 289: Validated Learning

Progress is measured in validated learning, not in features shipped. Many startups confuse vanity metrics (page views, downloads) with progress.

Ask yourself: What hypothesis did I validate or invalidate this week?
